incorporations.reason: Italian "balsamica" specifically connotes healing/medicinal qualities essential to the passage about consumptives seeking curative air; "balsamic" preserves this period-appropriate meaning confirmed by Edgren, whereas "balmy" (mild/pleasant) loses the therapeutic connotation.

incorporations.original: in arranging some ornament or other
incorporations.reason: Italian "ninnolo" specifically means a small decorative trinket/knick-knack, not a general ornament. Gemini's rendering is more precise.

incorporations.replacement: as a reporter wizened in the trade
incorporations.reason: Italian "raggrinzito nel mestiere" literally means wrinkled/wizened in the trade — a wry, self-deprecating image that "seasoned" flattens into a cliché. Gemini preserves the author's ironic self-portrait.

incorporations.original: as mute witnesses to an unheard-of crime
incorporations.replacement: as mute accusers of an unheard-of crime
incorporations.reason: Italian "denunciatrici" means accusers/denouncers, not witnesses. The sleeves are personified as bringing a charge against the Major, not merely observing.

incorporations.original: all the while chatting, ended by leading me
incorporations.replacement: dissembling all the while, ended by leading me
incorporations.reason: Italian "celando" means concealing/dissembling (from celare). The primary draft mistranslated this as "chatting" (which would be "cicalando" or "chiacchierando"). Signora Elisa was hiding her intention of leading Crespi to the courtyard, not chattering.

edgren_influences.edgren_definition: aria — listed under aria entry; "balsamic" retains sense of healing/soothing properties
edgren_influences.note: Edgren-era English "balsamic" meant having curative/soothing properties, precisely the connotation for consumptives seeking healing air. Modern English would default to "balmy" (mild/pleasant), losing the medicinal sense central to the passage.

edgren_influences.edgren_definition: appassire: wither (fade); become stale or old. appassito: withered; stale (old)
edgren_influences.note: Edgren confirms "withered" as the primary sense of "appassito", supporting the translation over a modern alternative like "wilted".
Edgren gives "put in mind" as the primary sense of rammentare, which is the exact phrasing used. A modern translator might default to "reminded me of".
Edgren confirms "immense" as the direct equivalent, supporting retention over alternatives like "vast".
Edgren confirms "rigid" as the primary sense, matching the translation.
